title: Offices of Certified Public Accountants

labor_share.method: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $68,045.5M / sectoral output (T30) $134,726.2M, year 2022
sources: BLS Industry Productivity and Costs flat files (ip.data.1.AllData), measures L02 and T30

occupations.title: Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $263,807 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541211); class <5: 35,096 firms, avg revenue/firm $300,013 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $46,952 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 9,158 firms, avg revenue/firm $944,405 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $147,799 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 4,257 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,000,227 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $313,036 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,015 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,602,856 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,033,347 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 271 firms, avg revenue/firm $37,129,554 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,810,775 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 88 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,137,349,114 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $177,995,136 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 2,286
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

breakdown_by_role.role: Accountants and Auditors (staff through senior manager) [13-2011]
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Audit fieldwork, workpaper preparation, tie-outs, sampling, tax return preparation and technical memo drafting are heavily exposed to current AI (extraction, drafting, reconciliation, research); professional-skepticism judgment, client walkthroughs, materiality calls and sign-off are not. Prompt-suggested band 0.3-0.5; midpoint 0.40 taken.

breakdown_by_role.role: Bookkeeping, Accounting, and Auditing Clerks (CAS write-up) [43-3031]
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Write-up, bank-feed reconciliation and monthly close are near-fully automatable by platform-native AI (QBO/Xero plus AI close tools); residual exception-handling and client contact remains.
Form-driven preparation from source documents automates (OCR + AI populate-and-check); review and signature migrate up to CPAs.

Licensure-gated opinions and return sign-off, client relationships and pricing judgment are the durable human layer; internal reporting, review-note workflow and engagement management automate — a bounded sliver.
Firm operations and practice management: scheduling, WIP/realization reporting and internal analytics automate; leadership, staffing and client escalation do not.
Scheduling, engagement letters, return assembly and client correspondence are cleanly automatable; front-desk/relationship tasks persist.
Document management, e-file transmittal, data entry — highly automatable administrative work.
Advisory/CAS-advisory work is AI-augmented (research, modeling, deliverable drafting) rather than replaced near-term; client-facing judgment dominates.
Invoicing, WIP-to-bill and collections workflows are near-fully automatable.
Supervision shrinks proportionally as the supervised admin base automates; people management persists.

breakdown_by_role.role: Long tail (IT, HR, other financial specialists, marketing; remainder of wage bill)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Mixed exposure: other financial specialists and marketing content partially automatable; IT/HR partially; allocated at a blended 0.30 rather than ignored per prompt guidance, since this tail is large enough to move V.

pi_dist.rationale: The firm owns the client relationship directly — no upstream gatekeeper, platform or procurement layer for the SMB/individual client base. But pricing-model deflation is real and line-specific: hourly and hours-anchored fixed fees (still a large share of compliance billing) convert AI hour-collapse into lower bills where clients benchmark fees; tax prep fees are visibly benchmarked; CAS is moving to fixed monthly subscriptions (79% of firms off hourly for bookkeeping) which retain savings; attest fees are payer-mandated (lenders/regulators require the opinion) and hold better. Counterweight: the documented CPA pipeline shortage has let firms push rates since 2023. Blended across attest/tax/CAS: 0.55 — meaningfully below the D2C-retail ceiling because the hourly/benchmarked share hands part of the AI saving to clients.

pi_moat.rationale: Licensure is a statutory moat for attest: audit opinions and many filings must be signed by a licensed CPA firm — clients cannot bypass with generic AI regardless of capability. Switching costs are high (institutional knowledge of the client's books, multi-year relationships), and the CPA supply shortage restricts entry. Deductions: simple-tax and bookkeeping revenue is genuinely bypassable (TurboTax full-service, QuickBooks Live, IRS Direct File expansion, generic AI for basic questions), and only the attest line carries the statutory floor; small-firm revenue is mostly tax/CAS, not attest.

t50_years.derivation: current_adoption_pct ~21% of tax/accounting firms already using GenAI (Thomson Reuters 2025), with Wolters Kluwer showing AI adoption 9% (2024) -> 41% (2025) and CPA.com/Blue J reporting tax-firm AI adoption nearly doubling in one year; 53% of firms planning use and only 25% with no plans. Historical analog: IRS e-file took ~15 years (1990 nationwide -> 50% of individual returns in 2005), but that required taxpayer behavior change and hardware; GenAI rides existing vendor platforms (CCH, Thomson Reuters, Intuit embed it) with the pipeline shortage forcing efficiency. At the observed doubling pace from ~21% material use, 50% material adoption in the automatable functions lands in ~2-4 years; 3.0 taken.

current_adoption_pct.source: Thomson Reuters 2025 Generative AI in Professional Services Report (tax firms); corroborated by Wolters Kluwer 2025 Future Ready Accountant report and CPA.com/Blue J survey
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: "21% of tax firms identified as already using GenAI technology, with 53% either planning to use the tech... only 25% of firms still have no current plans"; Wolters Kluwer: "AI adoption has surged — rising from 9% in 2024 to 41% in 2025... 35% already using it daily". Material = GenAI actively used in production workflows (research, prep, drafting, close), not experimentation; the TR 21% is the stricter firm-level measure and is used.

historical_analogs.value: IRS e-file: nationwide launch 1990, crossed 50% of individual returns in 2005 — ~15 years to majority. Cloud accounting (QBO/Xero, mid-2000s launch) took roughly a decade to majority use among small firms. Both analogs required client/infrastructure change; GenAI embeds directly into incumbent vendor suites already deployed at firms, so diffusion is running several times faster (9%->41% in one year per Wolters Kluwer).
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: "The e-file program expanded nationwide in 1990 and 4.2 million tax returns were filed"; "In 2005, e-filed returns crossed the 50 percent threshold, with 68.4 million returns filed"

owners_60plus_pct.source: Rosenberg National Survey of CPA Firm Statistics (via CPA Journal State of the Profession and CPA Practice Advisor succession coverage)
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: "The percentage of partners older than 60 is holding at approximately 25%... For smaller firms, that number is above 40%"; "65 percent of partners are over the age of 50". The 541211 firm base (50,885 firms, 35,096 with <5 employees) skews heavily to the small-firm tier where the 60+ share exceeds 40%; blended owner-weighted estimate 33%.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Rosenberg Survey: "A shortage of experienced, partner-ready staff is a perennial problem that firms seem unable to solve"; AICPA Trends documents the CPA pipeline shortage (declining accounting graduates and CPA exam candidates), and CPA Journal/CPA Practice Advisor report most partners lack quality talent to replace them.

active_consolidators.source: CPA Trendlines / Cornerstone PE Deal Tracker (2020-2026); Capstone Partners Accounting Services Market Update
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Tracker records "over 50 distinct private equity sponsors active in accounting transactions since 2024" and "180 deals in 2025, and more than 70 so far in 2026"; add-ons dominated 2023-2024 (Alpine/Ascend 14 transactions, DFW Capital and Thrive/Bessemer 16+ each, Charlesbank/Aprio, Blackstone/Citrin Cooperman 7). Conservative count of distinct PE-backed operating platforms actively doing add-ons in the last 24 months: 30.

buy_mult.source: Auxo Capital Advisors, Accounting Firm Valuation Multiples: 2026 Guide
buy_mult.figure_quoted: "Traditional local CPA practices: often about 0.8x-1.3x revenue or 3.0x-5.0x normalized EBITDA"; "Multi-partner CPA firms: often about 4.0x-6.5x normalized EBITDA". Median for single-location/small firms taken at 4.0x normalized EBITDA — explicitly on a normalized (market-rate owner comp) basis per rule 3; small-practice revenue/SDE quotes (0.6x-1.1x revenue, 1.8x-3.2x SDE) convert to a similar 3-5x range once market-rate owner comp is charged.

exit_mult.source: CT Acquisitions CPA and Accounting Firm M&A Multiples Report 2026; Capstone Partners; Auxo PE guide
exit_mult.figure_quoted: "The 2025 through Q2 2026 rebase pinned platform-tier multiples in a 10.0x to 15.0x adjusted EBITDA range"; "large APS platforms still trade... roughly ten to twelve times for high-quality firms". 11.0x taken as the credible mid-low platform mark for $10M+ EBITDA firms.

pricing_structure.value: Mixed: hourly and hours-anchored fixed fees dominate compliance (audit and tax engagements quoted off estimated hours with realization tracked); CAS/bookkeeping has shifted to fixed monthly subscription packages (79% of firms off hourly for bookkeeping); value pricing and advisory retainers growing but still minority. No cost-plus or third-party-audited fee schedules; fees are client-benchmarked, especially for commodity tax work.
pricing_structure.source: Karbon Accounting Services Pricing Guide; CPA Trendlines Cornerstone billing-rates report; SmartVault/SmartPath pricing survey coverage
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: "79% of firms have moved away from hourly billing for bookkeeping, shifting to fixed or tiered monthly packages"; "Value-based pricing, fixed-fee arrangements, and advisory retainers are replacing hourly billing" while hourly remains standard for complex/one-off engagements at "$150 to $400 per hour"

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw of 0.1912 implies ~19.1pp of revenue as maximum EBITDA uplift at full capture — far above the 3pp floor — while A = 3.33; no inconsistency. Even at realistic capture (C/10 ~ 0.385), retained uplift is ~7.4pp, comfortably material.

terminal_value.justification: By revenue line: attest is DURABLE by statute — audit opinions, comfort letters and many filings require a licensed CPA firm signature, a demand floor AI cannot remove; complex tax is DURABLE-to-PRESSURED (liability and complexity keep clients paying; the simple-return tail leaks to TurboTax/IRS Direct File/generic AI, but that tail sits mostly in 541213 tax-prep chains, not CPA offices); CAS is PRESSURED on price as platform-native AI compresses bookkeeping hours, though the outsourced-controller relationship persists. AI cuts the operator's cost of production (fieldwork/prep hours) far more than it replaces the product; the CPA shortage and value-pricing shift support fee retention. Net: smaller-headcount stamp-and-judgment economics with demand intact — DURABLE, with the caveat that hourly-billed compliance revenue deflates where clients benchmark fees (reflected in pi_dist = 0.55).

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Confirmed: V is built from the provided labor_share and role_mix plus task-level automatable fractions (fieldwork/prep/drafting vs judgment/sign-off); A is derived from Thomson Reuters/Wolters Kluwer/CPA.com adoption surveys and the e-file diffusion analog. The PE wave (deal tracker, platform counts, multiples) was used only for active_consolidators (B) and buy/exit multiples (M), never as evidence for V or A.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix: hourly and hours-anchored fixed fees dominate audit/tax compliance; CAS mostly fixed monthly subscription (79% of firms off hourly for bookkeeping); value pricing growing. Hourly/benchmarked compliance billing is a genuine deflation channel — AI hour-collapse becomes lower bills where clients compare fees — partially offset by the documented CPA pipeline shortage letting firms hold rates and by subscription/value pricing retaining savings. Reflected in pi_dist = 0.55 (vs ~0.8+ for unbenchmarked D2C pricing), with pi_moat = 0.7 carrying the statutory attest floor; the deflation is priced per revenue line as required.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A = 10/t50 spans 2.0 (t50=5) to 5.0 (t50=2) around the central 3.33; S moves roughly -0.53/+0.45 around 5.37 (S ~ A^0.2).
C spans 3.15-4.9; S spans roughly 5.16-5.65. Whether the pipeline shortage or fee benchmarking wins determines capture — the core economic uncertainty of the thesis.
M = 4*(11-buy)/buy spans 4.8 (buy=5.0) to 10.0-clamped (buy=3.0); S spans roughly 4.98-5.79.
OW (with bonus) spans 0.529-1.0; B spans ~5.45-7.49; S moves roughly -0.19/+0.15.

reviewer_note: Role-mix contamination handled per rule 6: the provided mix is 4-digit 5412, diluting CPA offices with tax-prep chains (541213), payroll bureaus (541214) and bookkeeping firms (541219) — within true CPA offices the accountant share is higher and clerk/preparer shares lower, which would push ai_replaceable_share modestly DOWN (clerk/preparer fractions are the highest); the provided mix was used unchanged for arithmetic. labor_share 0.5051 is IPS-based and understates true labor cost at partnership firms because LLP partner profit draws sit largely outside measured compensation — normalized-comp V_raw would be somewhat higher; conflict noted, provided value used. buy_mult converts broker revenue/SDE quotes to normalized EBITDA per rule 3 using Auxo's explicit normalized-EBITDA bands. exit_mult 11.0x is mid-low of the documented 10-15x platform band. n_band=2286 uses a generic 15.65% margin; true normalized margins at $2-10M-revenue CPA firms (after market-rate partner comp) are plausibly 15-25%, so the band is roughly right but the 20-99 employee class boundary is margin-sensitive. Formula-vs-intuition: no override needed — V=7.65, A=3.33 match the qualitative picture of a heavily AI-exposed, fast-adopting professional service whose capture (C=3.85) is the binding constraint.
